summary: Gruber assembles a timeline of Apple's relationship with the Trump administration, centering on Tim Cook's obsequious Oval Office visit where he presented Trump with a gold-and-glass trophy alongside a $600 billion US manufacturing commitment. He contextualizes this against Cook's earlier refusal to join Trump's Middle East tour (which praised Saudi Crown Prince MBS), Trump's retaliatory tariff threats, and the broader pattern of presidential favor-trading. Gruber argues the real story isn't Cook's moral failure but the corruption of the system itself — that Trump has turned governance into a protection racket where companies must pay tribute or face punishment. He concludes that the Oval Office spectacle is an embarrassment not for Cook alone but for the entire nation.
key_points: Trump operates a naughty-and-nice list for corporations: flattery and fealty earn tariff exemptions, while defiance triggers retaliatory tariffs and public attacks

Cook's Oval Office performance was gross but strategically rational — US manufacturing represents genuine alignment between Apple's interests and Trump's demands, unlike the Middle East tour praising MBS
The corruption of American governance isn't happening — it has already happened, and no company, however large, can serve as a check on that power
Apple's $600 billion investment plan is largely continuous with its Biden-era commitments; only the theatrical fanfare has changed
Faulting Cook individually is the 'easy way out' that avoids confronting the systemic problem
money_quotes: It's not even a case of 'kiss the ring and receive preferential treatment'. It's more a case of 'kiss his ass or else you'll face punishment'.

Faulting Cook for playing ball and kissing Trump's ring is like faulting a local business for paying into a protection racket — one in which the cops are complicit, and the whole scam exists at the behest of a crooked mayor.
There is no authority to appeal to for help when the highest office in the country is running the protection racket.
Trump, like a perverted Santa Claus, keeps only two lists: naughty and nice.
The corruption isn't happening, but happened. The United States isn't heading for existential trouble. We're in it — and a pathway out is not yet clear.
You can choose to play a crooked game straight, but you can't win.
The embarrassment of this week's Oval Office bending of the knee isn't Cook's or Apple's alone. It's an embarrassment for our entire nation. It's not him. It's not them. It's us.
A loser who played above reproach is still a loser.
key_insight: The spectacle of Tim Cook debasing himself before Trump in the Oval Office isn't evidence of one CEO's moral failure — it's proof that American governance has already been corrupted into a protection racket from which no company, however powerful, can opt out.
